1	---
2	name: blueprint-clean
3	description: Post-write blueprint gate. Strips Lean leakage, project history, and verbosity. Validates and inserts missing source quotes from PDFs/TeXs.
4	write_domain: "blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex"
5	read_only: false
6	can_spawn: true
7	default_enabled: true
8	dispatcher_notes: |
9	  - Dispatch this subagent AFTER a blueprint-writer round and BEFORE dispatching provers.
10	  - Required to ensure blueprint purity (math-only).
11	  - Include `references/**` in its write-domain so it can spawn `reference-retriever` if needed.
12	---
13	
14	# Blueprint Clean Subagent
15	
16	Your job is to read and refine a blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<slug>.tex`) to ensure it meets strict quality, purity, and citation standards.
17	
18	## Your Tasks
19	
20	1. **Strip Lean Leakage & Project History:**
21	   - Remove any mention of Lean tactics, typeclass notes, or specific Lean implementation strategies.
22	   - Remove project history references like "since iteration N", "our failed route", etc.
23	   - Ensure the blueprint reads as a timeless, standalone mathematical document.
24	2. **Trim Verbosity:**
25	   - Remove useless, overly conversational paragraphs.
26	   - Remove passages that are irrelevant or redundantly discuss things already implemented, unless they serve a mathematical purpose.
27	   - Keep the prose concise and mathematically precise.
28	3. **Enforce Citation Discipline & Add Quotes:**
29	   - Verify every block derived from a reference has a `% SOURCE QUOTE:` and `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`.
30	   - **If the quote is missing** but the reference exists in `references/`, you MUST open the actual reference `.pdf` or `.tex` file (using the `.md` index file as a pointer to find the section/page), extract the verbatim quote, and add it to the blueprint.
31	   - **If a reference SHOULD be cited** but isn't mentioned by the writer, and the source is missing from `references/`, spawn a `reference-retriever` to fetch it.
32	4. **Fix LaTeX:**
33	   - Correct basic LaTeX syntax errors.
34	   - Ensure `\uses{}` and `\label{}` cross-references are formatted correctly.
35	
36	## Spawning a Retriever
37	If you identify a missing reference, spawn it using:
38	```bash
39	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py --name reference-retriever --slug <slug> --directive-file <file> --write-domain 'references/**'
40	```
41	
42	Return your outcome and the path to your report.
43

1	\chapter{The sheaf-of-modules over-equivalence (shared slice root)}
2	\label{chap:Picard_SheafOverEquivalence}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/SheafOverEquivalence.lean
4	
5	\section{The equivalence}
6	\label{sec:soe_equivalence}
7	
8	\begin{definition}
9	
10	\leanok
11	[Sheaf-of-modules over-equivalence]
12	  \label{def:sheafofmodules_over_equivalence}
13	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.overEquivalence}
14	  % SOURCE: Mathlib code-location provenance (this is an Archon-original assembly
15	  % of existing Mathlib primitives, NOT an external literature citation, so it
16	  % carries no verbatim mathematical SOURCE QUOTE). The primitive instantiated is
17	  % SheafOfModules.pushforwardPushforwardEquivalence, defined at
18	  % Mathlib/Algebra/Category/ModuleCat/Sheaf/PushforwardContinuous.lean:305
19	  % (section Equivalence, lines 289--329): "equivalence of SheafOfModules
20	  % categories from a site equivalence e : C \simeq D together with a morphism of
21	  % the two structure ring sheaves and its inverse, with two coherences". The
22	  % underlying site equivalence is TopologicalSpace.Opens.overEquivalence
23	  % (Mathlib/Topology/Sheaves/Over.lean:41), and the continuity prerequisites are
24	  % the documented TODO at Over.lean:19--22.
25	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(U \subseteq X\) an open subset, with associated open
26	  immersion \(\iota : \widetilde{U} \hookrightarrow X\). The reindexing site
27	  equivalence
28	  \[
29	    e \;:=\; \mathtt{Opens.overEquivalence}\,U
30	       \;:\; \mathtt{Over}\,U \;\simeq\; \mathtt{Opens}\,\widetilde{U}
31	  \]
32	  --- between the slice of \(\mathtt{Opens}\,X\) over \(U\) (carrying the slice
33	  topology \((\mathtt{Opens.grothendieckTopology}\,X).\mathtt{over}\,U\)) and the
34	  opens of the subspace \(\widetilde{U}\) (carrying
35	  \(\mathtt{Opens.grothendieckTopology}\,\widetilde{U}\)) --- lifts to an
36	  \emph{equivalence of sheaf-of-modules categories}
37	  \[
38	    \mathtt{overEquivalence}\;:\;
39	      \mathtt{SheafOfModules}\,\bigl((\widetilde{U}).\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\bigr)
40	        \;\;\simeq\;\;
41	      \mathtt{SheafOfModules}\,\bigl(X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}.\mathtt{over}\,U\bigr),
42	  \]
43	  the modules-level lift of \(e\) that transports the structure ring sheaf. It is
44	  obtained by instantiating
45	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.pushforwardPushforwardEquivalence}\) at \(e\), with the
46	  open-immersion structure-sheaf ring morphism
47	  \[
48	    \varphi \;:\;
49	      X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}.\mathtt{over}\,U
50	      \;\longrightarrow\;
51	      \bigl(e.\mathtt{functor}.\mathtt{sheafPushforwardContinuous}\bigr).\mathrm{obj}\,
52	        \bigl((\widetilde{U}).\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\bigr)
53	  \]
54	  --- sectionwise at an object \(V \in \mathtt{Over}\,U\) the open-immersion
55	  comparison \(\mathcal{O}_X(V.\mathrm{left}) \to \mathcal{O}_{\widetilde{U}}(e\,V)\),
56	  i.e.\ the section-level isomorphism \(\iota.\mathtt{appIso}\) of the structure
57	  sheaves --- together with its inverse \(\psi\) and the two coherences
58	  \(H_1, H_2\) expressing that \(\varphi\) and \(\psi\) are mutually inverse.
59	\end{definition}
60	
61	\begin{proof}
62	  The construction is the assembly of three existing Mathlib primitives; the only
63	  step carrying mathematical content is the ring morphism \(\varphi\).
64	
65	  \emph{Continuity (no work).} The equivalence
66	  \(\mathtt{pushforwardPushforwardEquivalence}\) requires both legs of the site
67	  equivalence \(e\) to be continuous:
68	  \(e.\mathtt{functor}\) and \(e.\mathtt{inverse}\). For a dense-subsite inclusion
69	  continuity is automatic --- it is a typeclass instance --- and for an
70	  equivalence the density of one leg propagates to the other. The declaration
71	  \(\mathtt{overEquivInverseIsDenseSubsite}\) exhibits \(e.\mathtt{inverse}\) as a
72	  dense subsite of the slice site, from which both continuity legs follow by
73	  inference. The Mathlib TODO at \texttt{Topology/Sheaves/Over.lean} (lines 19--22)
74	  is discharged for this case.
75	
76	  \emph{The ring morphism \(\varphi\) (the genuine content).} The two structure
77	  ring sheaves are different objects on different sites:
78	  \(X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}.\mathtt{over}\,U\) over \(\mathtt{Over}\,U\), and
79	  \((\widetilde{U}).\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\) over \(\mathtt{Opens}\,\widetilde{U}\).
80	  The open immersion \(\iota\) identifies them: at an object \(V \in
81	  \mathtt{Over}\,U\) the sections \(\mathcal{O}_X(V.\mathrm{left})\) and
82	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\widetilde{U}}(\iota^{-1}(V.\mathrm{left}))\) agree through the
83	  open-immersion section isomorphism \(\iota.\mathtt{appIso}\). This is verbatim the
84	  datum that \(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.restrictFunctor}\) already uses inline
85	  (its structure map is \((\iota.\mathtt{appIso}\,\cdot).\mathrm{inv}\)). Packaging
86	  this section-level isomorphism naturally in \(V\) yields \(\varphi\); its inverse
87	  is \(\psi\), and the coherences \(H_1, H_2\) record that the
88	  \(\iota.\mathtt{appIso}\) round-trips are identities, proved by the
89	  inverse-iso laws (\(\mathtt{Sheaf.hom\_ext}\) reduces them sectionwise).
90	
91	  Feeding \(e\), \(\varphi\), \(\psi\), \(H_1\), \(H_2\) into
92	  \(\mathtt{pushforwardPushforwardEquivalence}\) produces the asserted equivalence.
93	  Its underlying functor is \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.pushforward}\,\varphi\), which
94	  is what the two consumer isomorphisms below unfold.
95	\end{proof}
96	
97	\section{The equivalence carries restriction and unit}
98	\label{sec:soe_consumers}
99	
100	\begin{lemma}
101	
102	\leanok
103	[The over-equivalence carries restriction to over]
104	  \label{lem:sheafofmodules_restrict_over_iso}
105	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.restrictOverIso}
106	  \uses{def:sheafofmodules_over_equivalence}
107	  Let \(M \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). Under the over-equivalence of
108	  \cref{def:sheafofmodules_over_equivalence} there is a canonical isomorphism
109	  \[
110	    \mathtt{overEquivalence}.\mathtt{functor}.\mathrm{obj}\,\bigl(M|_\iota\bigr)
111	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
112	    M.\mathtt{over}\,U,
113	  \]
114	  i.e.\ the equivalence carries the restriction \(M|_\iota\) of \(M\) along the
115	  open immersion to the slice object \(M.\mathtt{over}\,U\).
116	\end{lemma}
117	
118	\begin{proof}
119	  \uses{def:sheafofmodules_over_equivalence}
120	  The restriction \(M|_\iota\) is \emph{itself} a pushforward of sheaves of
121	  modules: by construction \(M|_\iota = \mathtt{SheafOfModules.pushforward}\)
122	  applied along \(\iota.\mathtt{opensFunctor}\) (the open-immersion functor
123	  \(\mathtt{Opens}\,\widetilde{U} \to \mathtt{Opens}\,X\)). The functor underlying
124	  the over-equivalence is \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.pushforward}\,\varphi\). Their
125	  composite is again a single pushforward, identified by
126	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.pushforwardComp}\), which here is the identity
127	  isomorphism \(\mathtt{Iso.refl}\). It remains to identify the two underlying
128	  index functors \(\mathtt{Over}\,U \to \mathtt{Opens}\,X\) entering the two
129	  pushforwards: both send an object \(V\) to its left component \(V.\mathrm{left}\),
130	  so they are equal, and \(\mathtt{pushforwardNatIso}\) along the \(\mathtt{eqToIso}\)
131	  of that equality transports the composite to \(M.\mathtt{over}\,U\). The whole
132	  argument mirrors, step for step, the existing
133	  \(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.restrictFunctorAdjCounitIso}\).
134	\end{proof}
135	
136	\begin{lemma}
137	
138	\leanok
139	[The over-equivalence carries unit to unit]
140	  \label{lem:sheafofmodules_unit_over_iso}
141	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.unitOverIso}
142	  \uses{def:sheafofmodules_over_equivalence}
143	  Under the over-equivalence of \cref{def:sheafofmodules_over_equivalence} there is
144	  a canonical isomorphism
145	  \[
146	    \mathtt{overEquivalence}.\mathtt{functor}.\mathrm{obj}\,
147	      \bigl(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.unit}\,(\widetilde{U}).\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\bigr)
148	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
149	    \mathtt{SheafOfModules.unit}\,\bigl(X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}.\mathtt{over}\,U\bigr),
150	  \]
151	  i.e.\ the equivalence carries the unit module (the structure sheaf-as-module) of
152	  the subspace site to the unit module of the slice site.
153	\end{lemma}
154	
155	\begin{proof}
156	  \uses{def:sheafofmodules_over_equivalence}
157	  The functor underlying the over-equivalence is
158	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.pushforward}\,\varphi\). The pushforward of a unit module
159	  along a morphism of ringed sites is the unit module of the target ring sheaf,
160	  transported along the ring identification \(\varphi\): the structure sheaf
161	  pushes forward to the structure sheaf because \(\varphi\) is precisely the
162	  open-immersion identification of the two structure ring sheaves. Concretely the
163	  pushforward-of-unit computation produces \(\mathtt{unit}\) of the codomain ring
164	  sheaf up to \(\varphi\), and \(\varphi\) being an isomorphism makes this a
165	  genuine isomorphism.
166	
167	  Concretely, the canonical comparison map realising this transport is the
168	  unit-to-pushforward-of-unit morphism
169	  \[
170	    \mathtt{SheafOfModules.unitToPushforwardObjUnit}\,\varphi
171	      \;:\;
172	      \mathtt{unit}\,\bigl(X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}.\mathtt{over}\,U\bigr)
173	      \;\longrightarrow\;
174	      (\mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}\,
175	        \bigl(\mathtt{unit}\,(\widetilde{U}).\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\bigr).
176	  \]
177	  Its section at an open \(W\) is exactly the section-level ring map
178	  \(\varphi.\mathrm{app}\,W\): the morphism acts on a section by multiplication
179	  through \(\varphi\), so on \(W\) it is the additive-group map underlying
180	  \(\varphi.\mathrm{app}\,W\) (the characterisation
181	  \(\mathtt{unitToPushforwardObjUnit\_val\_app\_apply}\)). Since \(\varphi\) is an
182	  isomorphism of ring sheaves, each component \(\varphi.\mathrm{app}\,W\) is a ring
183	  isomorphism, hence its underlying additive-group map is an isomorphism.
184	
185	  It follows that \(\mathtt{unitToPushforwardObjUnit}\,\varphi\) is an isomorphism.
186	  One reflects the sectionwise isomorphism upward through the two forgetful
187	  functors --- first \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.forget}\) (down to presheaves of
188	  modules), then \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.toPresheaf}\) (down to presheaves of
189	  additive groups) --- so that being an isomorphism reduces to the componentwise
190	  isomorphism check
191	  \(\mathtt{NatTrans.isIso\_iff\_isIso\_app}\) on the underlying natural
192	  transformation, which is the sectionwise statement just established. The desired
193	  isomorphism \(\mathtt{unitOverIso}\) is then the inverse of the resulting
194	  isomorphism, \((\mathtt{asIso}\,(\mathtt{unitToPushforwardObjUnit}\,\varphi))^{-1}\).
195	  This is the slice-site analogue of
196	  \(\mathtt{pullbackObjUnitToUnitIso}\).
197	\end{proof}
198	
199	\section{The engine corollary}
200	\label{sec:soe_chart}
201	
202	\begin{lemma}
203	
204	\leanok
205	[Over--restrict trivialisation bridge]
206	  \label{lem:chart_over_iso}
207	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.chartOverIso}
208	  \uses{lem:sheafofmodules_restrict_over_iso, lem:sheafofmodules_unit_over_iso}
209	  Let \(M \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) and \(U \subseteq X\) open, and suppose
210	  given a trivialisation of the restriction along the open immersion
211	  \(\iota : \widetilde{U} \hookrightarrow X\),
212	  \[
213	    e \;:\; M|_\iota \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
214	      \mathtt{SheafOfModules.unit}\,(\widetilde{U}).\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}.
215	  \]
216	  Then there is a canonical isomorphism in the slice category
217	  \[
218	    \mathtt{chartOverIso}\,M\,U\,e \;:\;
219	      M.\mathtt{over}\,U \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
220	      \mathtt{SheafOfModules.unit}\,\bigl(X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}.\mathtt{over}\,U\bigr).
221	  \]
222	\end{lemma}
223	
224	\begin{proof}
225	  \uses{lem:sheafofmodules_restrict_over_iso, lem:sheafofmodules_unit_over_iso,
226	        def:sheafofmodules_over_equivalence}
227	  Transport the trivialisation \(e\) across the over-equivalence and reconcile the
228	  two ends with the consumer isomorphisms. Explicitly, \(\mathtt{chartOverIso}\,M\,U\,e\)
229	  is the composite
230	  \[
231	    M.\mathtt{over}\,U
232	      \xrightarrow[\sim]{\ (\mathtt{restrictOverIso})^{-1}\ }
233	      \mathtt{overEquivalence}.\mathtt{functor}.\mathrm{obj}\,\bigl(M|_\iota\bigr)
234	      \xrightarrow[\sim]{\ \mathtt{overEquivalence}.\mathtt{functor}.\mathtt{mapIso}\,e\ }
235	      \mathtt{overEquivalence}.\mathtt{functor}.\mathrm{obj}\,(\mathtt{unit}\,\cdots)
236	      \xrightarrow[\sim]{\ \mathtt{unitOverIso}\ }
237	      \mathtt{unit}\,\bigl(X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}.\mathtt{over}\,U\bigr).
238	  \]
239	  The first arrow is the inverse of \cref{lem:sheafofmodules_restrict_over_iso}; the
240	  middle arrow is the image of \(e\) under the (functor of the) over-equivalence,
241	  which is functorial in isomorphisms hence sends the iso \(e\) to an iso; the last
242	  arrow is \cref{lem:sheafofmodules_unit_over_iso}. Each factor is an isomorphism,
243	  so the composite is.
244	
245	  This lemma addresses the site mismatch in the chart-presentation of
246	  \cref{lem:lbc_chart_presentation}: the given trivialisation \(e\) lives in
247	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules}\,(\widetilde{U}).\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\) (the open
248	  subspace site), whereas the finite-presentation transport
249	  \(\mathtt{Presentation.ofIsIso}\) must act in
250	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules}\,(X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}.\mathtt{over}\,U)\) (the slice
251	  site); \(\mathtt{chartOverIso}\) crosses that gap. It is stated in full
252	  generality in \(M\), \(U\), \(e\) for use in
253	  \cref{chap:Picard_LineBundleCoherence}. The over-equivalence of
254	  \cref{def:sheafofmodules_over_equivalence} likewise underlies the internal-Hom
255	  theory: the commutativity of the internal Hom with the slice-site change of base
256	  (\cref{lem:dual_restrict_iso}) passes through the same equivalence.
257	\end{proof}
258

1	\chapter{Coherence of locally trivial line bundles}
2	\label{chap:Picard_LineBundleCoherence}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/LineBundleCoherence.lean
4	
5	% STRATEGY NOTE. This chapter supplies the cheap, on-path half of the
6	% line-bundle coherence requirement of the A.2.c Quot-scheme embedding
7	% (\cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}, \cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability}).
8	% The Quot functor takes coherent quotients of a coherent sheaf; to feed it a
9	% line bundle one must know that the line bundle is itself coherent, i.e. a
10	% finitely presented \(\mathcal{O}\)-module. The embedding's input is a point of
11	% the relative Picard scheme \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\), whose carrier is the
12	% \emph{locally trivial} predicate \(\texttt{IsLocallyTrivial}\) of
13	% \cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback} (the project-side stand-in for an
14	% invertible sheaf, a sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules locally isomorphic to
15	% \(\mathcal{O}\) of rank one). The chapter proves
16	% \(\texttt{IsLocallyTrivial} M \Rightarrow M.\mathtt{IsFinitePresentation}\),
17	% together with a rank-one / flatness record. The general implication
18	% ``\(M\) tensor-invertible \(\Rightarrow\) \(M\) locally free of rank one''
19	% (the converse spreading-out, Stacks Lemma~0B8M when stalks are local) is
20	% \emph{not} crossed here: the carrier is already locally trivial, so the
21	% finite-presentation spreading-out is never needed.
22	
23	\section{Setup and motivation}
24	\label{sec:lbc_setup}
25	
26	Fix a scheme \(X\) and write \(X.\mathtt{Modules}\) for the category of sheaves
27	of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules (Mathlib's \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules}\) on the
28	ringed-space site of \(X\), with structure sheaf \(X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\)).
29	A \emph{line bundle} on \(X\) is an invertible \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module; in the
30	sense of \cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback} we model line bundles by the
31	predicate \(\texttt{IsLocallyTrivial}\): a module \(M : X.\mathtt{Modules}\) is locally
32	trivial of rank one if every point \(x \in X\) has an \emph{affine} open
33	neighbourhood \(U\) on which the restriction \(M|_U\) is isomorphic, as an
34	\(\mathcal{O}_U\)-module, to the structure sheaf-as-module \(\struct{U}\):
35	\[
36	  \texttt{IsLocallyTrivial}(M) \;:=\;
37	  \forall x \in X,\ \exists\, U \ni x \text{ affine open},\quad
38	  M|_U \;\cong\; \struct{U}.
39	\]
40	This is the rank-one case of Stacks Definition~17.25.1
41	(Tag~01CS, invertible \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules), and
42	agrees with the classical ``locally free of rank one'' description by Stacks
43	Lemma~17.25.4 (Tag~0B8M).
44	
45	The goal of the chapter is to record that such a module is \emph{coherent} in the
46	sense the Quot construction consumes, namely finitely presented:
47	\(M.\mathtt{IsFinitePresentation}\) in Mathlib's
48	\(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.IsFinitePresentation}\) (built from a
49	\(\mathtt{QuasicoherentData}\)). The mathematics is elementary: on each
50	trivialising chart \(M|_U \cong \struct{U}\) is finite free of rank one, hence
51	has the trivial finite presentation, and finite presentation is by definition a
52	local condition. No stalk computation and no spreading-out are involved. The
53	content is the bookkeeping that turns the pointwise trivialisation into the
54	indexed \(\mathtt{QuasicoherentData}\) Mathlib expects.
55	
56	\section{Site instances for quasi-coherent data}
57	\label{sec:lbc_site_instances}
58	
59	% NOTE (chief unknown). Mathlib's SheafOfModules.IsFinitePresentation /
60	% QuasicoherentData are stated under three site hypotheses on the slice
61	% topologies J.over X of the underlying site J of X.ringCatSheaf:
62	%   [∀ x, (J.over x).HasSheafCompose (forget₂ RingCat AddCommGrpCat)]
63	%   [∀ x, HasWeakSheafify (J.over x) AddCommGrpCat]
64	%   [∀ x, (J.over x).WEqualsLocallyBijective AddCommGrpCat]
65	% These must be discharged (or assumed as instances) before the statements in
66	% this chapter typecheck for X.ringCatSheaf. The project already lives in the
67	% SheafOfModules X.ringCatSheaf world, so these should resolve; this is the one
68	% item to verify when the Lean file is scaffolded.
69	
70	The Mathlib predicates \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.IsFinitePresentation}\) and
71	\(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.QuasicoherentData}\) are stated relative to a
72	Grothendieck topology \(J\) on a base category \(C\) and a sheaf of rings
73	\(R : \Sheaf(J, \RingCat)\). They require, for every object \(x : C\), three
74	instances on the slice topology \(J/x\) (Mathlib \(J.\mathtt{over}\ x\)):
75	\[
76	  (J/x).\mathtt{HasSheafCompose}(\forget_{\RingCat \to \AddCommGrpCat}),\quad
77	  \HasWeakSheafify(J/x, \AddCommGrpCat),\quad
78	  (J/x).\mathtt{WEqualsLocallyBijective}(\AddCommGrpCat).
79	\]
80	For \(X.\mathtt{Modules} = \mathtt{SheafOfModules}\ X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\)
81	these are the ambient site instances of the ringed space underlying \(X\). We
82	record their availability as a standing assumption for the chapter; verifying it
83	for \(X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\) is the single instance-resolution check the
84	formalisation must clear at entry, and is independent of the mathematical
85	content below.
86	
87	\begin{remark}
88	  \label{rem:lbc_site_instances}
89	  For the ringed-space site underlying \(X\) with sheaf of rings
90	  \(R = X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\), every slice topology \(J/x\) carries the
91	  three instances \(\mathtt{HasSheafCompose}\), \(\HasWeakSheafify\) and
92	  \(\mathtt{WEqualsLocallyBijective}\) (for the forgetful functor
93	  \(\RingCat \to \AddCommGrpCat\)) demanded by
94	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.QuasicoherentData}\). Under this assumption the
95	  predicate \(M.\mathtt{IsFinitePresentation}\) is well-formed for every
96	  \(M : X.\mathtt{Modules}\).
97	\end{remark}
98	
99	\section{From pointwise triviality to a trivialising cover}
100	\label{sec:lbc_cover}
101	
102	\begin{lemma}\leanok
103	[Trivialising affine cover]
104	  \label{lem:lbc_trivializing_cover}
105	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial.exists_trivializing_cover}
106	  \uses{def:line_bundle_on_product}
107	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Modules on Ringed Spaces, Definition 17.25.1
108	  % "invertible module" (Tag 01CS), Section 17.25 (Tag 01CR)
109	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L4046-L4059).
110	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
111	  % "Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space. An
112	  %  {\it invertible $\mathcal{O}_X$-module} is a sheaf
113	  %  of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules $\mathcal{L}$ such that
114	  %  the functor
115	  %  $$
116	  %  \textit{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_X) \longrightarrow \textit{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_X),\quad
117	  %  \mathcal{F} \longmapsto \mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{F}
118	  %  $$
119	  %  is an equivalence of categories. We say that $\mathcal{L}$ is
120	  %  {\it trivial} if it is isomorphic as an $\mathcal{O}_X$-module
121	  %  to $\mathcal{O}_X$."
122	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag~01CS (Modules, Definition~17.25.1).}
123	  Let \(M : X.\mathtt{Modules}\) be locally trivial, \(\texttt{IsLocallyTrivial}(M)\).
124	  Then there exist an index set \(I\), a family of affine open subschemes
125	  \(\{U_i\}_{i \in I}\) of \(X\) covering \(X\) (i.e. \(\bigcup_i U_i = X\)), and
126	  for each \(i\) an isomorphism of \(\mathcal{O}_{U_i}\)-modules
127	  \[
128	    e_i \;:\; M|_{U_i} \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; \struct{U_i}
129	  \]
130	  to the structure sheaf-as-module on \(U_i\).
131	\end{lemma}
132	
133	\begin{proof}
134	  \uses{def:line_bundle_on_product}
135	  \leanok
136	  Apply the hypothesis \(\texttt{IsLocallyTrivial}(M)\) at every point \(x \in X\): it
137	  produces an affine open \(U_x \ni x\) and an isomorphism
138	  \(e_x : M|_{U_x} \cong \struct{U_x}\). Take the index set \(I := X\) (the
139	  underlying set of points), and to the index \(x\) assign the chosen
140	  neighbourhood \(U_x\) and the chosen isomorphism \(e_x\). The family
141	  \(\{U_x\}_{x \in X}\) covers \(X\) because each \(x\) lies in its own \(U_x\).
142	  This is exactly the data the definition supplies, packaged as an indexed
143	  family rather than a pointwise existential.
144	\end{proof}
145	
146	\section{The trivial presentation on a chart}
147	\label{sec:lbc_chart_presentation}
148	
149	\begin{lemma}\leanok
150	[Finite free presentation on a chart]
151	  \label{lem:lbc_chart_presentation}
152	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial.chartPresentation}
153	  \uses{lem:lbc_trivializing_cover}
154	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Modules on Ringed Spaces, section
155	  % "Modules of finite presentation" (\label{definition-finite-presentation})
156	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L1377-L1392).
157	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
158	  % "We say that $\mathcal{F}$ is of {\it finite presentation}
159	  %  if for every point $x \in X$ there exists an open neighbourhood
160	  %  $x\in U \subset X$, and  $n, m \in \mathbf{N}$ such that $\mathcal{F}|_U$
161	  %  is isomorphic to the cokernel of a map
162	  %  $$
163	  %  \bigoplus\nolimits_{j = 1, \ldots, m}
164	  %  \mathcal{O}_U
165	  %  \longrightarrow
166	  %  \bigoplus\nolimits_{i = 1, \ldots, n}
167	  %  \mathcal{O}_U
168	  %  $$"
169	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Modules, ``Modules of finite
170	  presentation''.}
171	  On each chart \(U_i\) of the trivialising cover of
172	  \cref{lem:lbc_trivializing_cover}, the restriction \(M|_{U_i}\) admits the
173	  trivial finite free presentation
174	  \[
175	    0 \;\longrightarrow\; \struct{U_i} \;\longrightarrow\; M|_{U_i}
176	    \;\longrightarrow\; 0,
177	  \]
178	  i.e. \(M|_{U_i}\) is the cokernel of the zero map
179	  \(\bigoplus_{j \in \varnothing} \struct{U_i} \to \struct{U_i}\)
180	  (the case \(m = 0\), \(n = 1\) of the finite-presentation pattern). The
181	  unit sheaf-as-module \(\struct{U_i}\) (Mathlib's \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.unit}\))
182	  carries a canonical free presentation on one generator with no relations;
183	  this presentation is finite.
184	\end{lemma}
185	
186	\begin{proof}
187	  \uses{lem:lbc_trivializing_cover}
188	  \leanok
189	  The structure sheaf-as-module \(\struct{U_i} = \mathtt{SheafOfModules.unit}\,
190	  (U_i).\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\) is free of rank one on the single generator
191	  \(1\): the identity map \(\struct{U_i} \to \struct{U_i}\) exhibits it as
192	  \(\bigoplus_{i \in \{*\}} \struct{U_i}\), so the surjection
193	  \(\struct{U_i}^{\oplus 1} \twoheadrightarrow \struct{U_i}\) is an isomorphism
194	  and its kernel is zero, generated by the empty family. Thus \(\struct{U_i}\)
195	  has the canonical presentation with \(n = 1\) generators and \(m = 0\)
196	  relations; this canonical \(\mathtt{unit}\)-presentation is the rank-one free
197	  presentation, and Mathlib records it as \emph{finite} (the
198	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.unit}\) finite-presentation instance).
199	
200	  The chart presentation is obtained by transporting the canonical finite
201	  presentation of \(\mathtt{unit}\,(U_i).\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\) along the
202	  isomorphism \(e_i\), using \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.Presentation.ofIsIso}\).
203	  This construction carries any finite presentation of a module to a finite
204	  presentation of any isomorphic module, so the transported presentation of
205	  \(M|_{U_i}\) is again finite.
206	  % NOTE (updated iter-259): the over<->restrict categorical bridge is provided
207	  % by the shared-root construction `Scheme.Modules.chartOverIso` (chapter
208	  % Picard_SheafOverEquivalence, lem:chart_over_iso). The local chartOverIso def
209	  % in LineBundleCoherence.lean is a redirect to it and carries no sorry; this
210	  % file is locally sorry-free. No bridge block is needed in this chapter.
211	\end{proof}
212	
213	\section{Finite presentation of a locally trivial line bundle}
214	\label{sec:lbc_finite_presentation}
215	
216	\begin{theorem}\leanok
217	[Locally trivial \(\Rightarrow\) finitely presented]
218	  \label{thm:lbc_isFinitePresentation}
219	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial.isFinitePresentation}
220	  \uses{lem:lbc_trivializing_cover, lem:lbc_chart_presentation, rem:lbc_site_instances}
221	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Modules on Ringed Spaces,
222	  % Lemma 17.25.4 (Tag 0B8M), invertible vs locally free of rank one
223	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L4159-L4165).
224	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
225	  % "Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space. Any locally free
226	  %  $\mathcal{O}_X$-module of rank $1$ is invertible.
227	  %  If all stalks $\mathcal{O}_{X, x}$ are local rings, then
228	  %  the converse holds as well (but in general this is not the case)."
229	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag~0B8M (Modules, Lemma~17.25.4).}
230	  Let \(X\) be a scheme satisfying \cref{rem:lbc_site_instances} and let
231	  \(M : X.\mathtt{Modules}\) be locally trivial, \(\texttt{IsLocallyTrivial}(M)\). Then
232	  \(M\) is finitely presented: \(M.\mathtt{IsFinitePresentation}\) holds in
233	  Mathlib's \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.IsFinitePresentation}\).
234	\end{theorem}
235	
236	\begin{proof}
237	  \uses{lem:lbc_trivializing_cover, lem:lbc_chart_presentation}
238	  \leanok
239	  By \cref{lem:lbc_trivializing_cover} choose a trivialising affine cover
240	  \(\{U_i\}_{i \in I}\) with isomorphisms \(e_i : M|_{U_i} \cong \struct{U_i}\).
241	  Assemble these into a \(\mathtt{QuasicoherentData}\) for \(M\): the index type
242	  is \(I\), the covering objects are the affine opens \(U_i\) (which cover \(X\)),
243	  and the presentation attached to index \(i\) is the finite free presentation of
244	  \(M|_{U_i}\) (equivalently, of the restriction \(M.\mathtt{over}\,U_i\))
245	  produced in \cref{lem:lbc_chart_presentation}. Each such presentation is
246	  finite by \cref{lem:lbc_chart_presentation}; the assembled
247	  \(\mathtt{QuasicoherentData}\) therefore satisfies
248	  \(\mathtt{QuasicoherentData.IsFinitePresentation}\), and the constructor
249	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.IsFinitePresentation.mk}\) yields
250	  \(M.\mathtt{IsFinitePresentation}\).
251	
252	  The only non-formal input is the trivialising cover; everything else is the
253	  definitional unwinding of finite presentation as a local property
254	  (Stacks, ``Modules of finite presentation''), since a rank-one free module is its own finite
255	  presentation. No stalk computation and no neighbourhood-spreading argument is
256	  used: the hypothesis already provides the local free models.
257	\end{proof}
258	
259	\begin{corollary}\leanok
260	[Locally trivial \(\Rightarrow\) finite type]
261	  \label{cor:lbc_isFiniteType}
262	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial.isFiniteType}
263	  \uses{thm:lbc_isFinitePresentation}
264	  Under the hypotheses of \cref{thm:lbc_isFinitePresentation}, the locally
265	  trivial module \(M\) is of finite type (\(M.\mathtt{IsFiniteType}\)). Quasi-coherence
266	  (\(M.\mathtt{IsQuasicoherent}\)) follows from finite presentation via
267	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.instIsQuasicoherentOfIsFinitePresentation}\).
268	\end{corollary}
269	
270	\begin{proof}
271	  \uses{thm:lbc_isFinitePresentation}
272	  \leanok
273	  Finite presentation implies finite type by Mathlib's
274	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.instIsFiniteTypeOfIsFinitePresentation}\), immediate
275	  from \cref{thm:lbc_isFinitePresentation}. Quasi-coherence is immediate from
276	  finite presentation by
277	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.instIsQuasicoherentOfIsFinitePresentation}\).
278	\end{proof}
279	
280	\section{Rank one and flatness record}
281	\label{sec:lbc_rank_flat}
282	
283	% NOTE. Mathlib (at the pin) has no SheafOfModules-level "locally free of rank r"
284	% or "flat" predicate (verified absent: no SheafOfModules.IsLocallyFree). The
285	% rank-one and flatness facts are therefore recorded chart-locally, as the data
286	% the Quot embedding actually consumes, rather than as instances of a missing
287	% global predicate. Project-side predicates may be introduced if a downstream
288	% consumer needs them packaged; this is flagged for the plan agent.
289	
290	\begin{lemma}\leanok
291	[Chart-local rank one and flatness]
292	  \label{lem:lbc_rank_flat}
293	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial.chart_free_rank_one}
294	  \uses{lem:lbc_trivializing_cover}
295	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Modules on Ringed Spaces, section
296	  % "Locally free sheaves" (\label{definition-locally-free}, "(finite)
297	  % locally free of rank $r$")
298	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L2079-L2094).
299	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
300	  % "\item We say $\mathcal{F}$ is {\it locally free} if for every
301	  %  point $x \in X$ there exist a set $I$ and an open
302	  %  neighbourhood $x \in U \subset X$
303	  %  such that $\mathcal{F}|_U$ is isomorphic to
304	  %  $\bigoplus_{i \in I} \mathcal{O}_X|_U$ as an $\mathcal{O}_X|_U$-module.
305	  %  \item We say $\mathcal{F}$ is {\it finite locally free} if we may
306	  %  choose the index sets $I$ to be finite.
307	  %  \item We say $\mathcal{F}$ is {\it finite locally free of rank $r$}
308	  %  if we may choose the index sets $I$ to have cardinality $r$."
309	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Modules, ``Locally free sheaves'' (finite
310	  locally free of rank \(r\)).}
311	  Let \(M : X.\mathtt{Modules}\) be locally trivial. The lemma records, at each
312	  point \(x \in X\), a chart \(U \ni x\) together with the trivialising iso
313	  \(M|_U \cong \struct{U}\) (to \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.unit}\)). This iso is the
314	  rank-one free \emph{model}: rank-one freeness and flatness are
315	  \emph{consequences} of this iso, because \(\struct{U}\) is the free
316	  \(\mathcal{O}_U\)-module of rank one and a flat module over itself (a ring is
317	  flat over itself), and both properties transport along \(e\). In the sense of
318	  \cref{def:line_bundle_on_product} this exhibits \(M\) as finite locally free
319	  of rank one and flat on the cover.
320	\end{lemma}
321	
322	\begin{proof}
323	  \uses{lem:lbc_trivializing_cover}
324	  \leanok
325	  Immediate from the trivialisation: on \(U_i\), \(e_i\) identifies \(M|_{U_i}\)
326	  with \(\struct{U_i} = \bigoplus_{i \in \{*\}} \struct{U_i}\), the free module
327	  of rank one. The structure sheaf-as-module is flat over itself, and an
328	  isomorphic module is flat; the index set \(\{*\}\) has cardinality one, so the
329	  rank is one. The properties are recorded chart-locally because rank-one
330	  freeness and flatness are local conditions, and the chart-local data are what
331	  the Quot-scheme embedding consumes when it forms the Hilbert-polynomial input
332	  on a fibre.
333	\end{proof}
334

# blueprint-clean report — bc259

## Scope
- `blueprint/src/chapters/Picard_SheafOverEquivalence.tex`
- `blueprint/src/chapters/Picard_LineBundleCoherence.tex`

## Findings and actions

### Picard_SheafOverEquivalence.tex — no changes required

The `unitOverIso` proof expansion (bw259-soe, lines 155–197) is clean:
- All Lean identifiers are inside `\mathtt{...}` (e.g.\ `\mathtt{unitToPushforwardObjUnit\_val\_app\_apply}`, `\mathtt{NatTrans.isIso\_iff\_isIso\_app}`).
- No raw tactic strings or `by ...` blocks.
- No iteration-narrative references.
- No fabricated `% SOURCE` lines. The pre-existing `% SOURCE:` block (lines 14–24) explicitly declares itself a Mathlib provenance note, not an external literature citation, and predates this iteration.
- `\leanok` markers untouched.

### Picard_LineBundleCoherence.tex — one NOTE comment fixed

**Issue**: The `% NOTE` comment introduced by bw259-lbc (inside the proof of `lem:lbc_chart_presentation`) contained two purity violations:
